sentences.text: My name now this Bertha Leverton-- B-E-R-T-H-A L-E-V-E-R-T-O-N.
It was Bertha or Berta Engelhard-- E-N-G-E-L-H-A-R-D.
23rd of January.
In Munich.
Munchen or Munich.
Which way do you want it?
M-U-N-I-C-H-- that's in Germany, south Germany.
I thought about that.
I think my very earliest memory, I must've been not much out of babyhood.
Because I remember I couldn't really talk properly.
And I must have been sleeping in a room by myself by then.
And I cried.
I screamed very much one night, because the moon was shining through the window.
And there was a bust in the room-- I can see it in front of me today-- a white alabaster bust of a lady, half bust.
And the moon shone on her.
And it must have terrified me.
And I was screaming and screaming.
And my mother came running in.
And I couldn't really explain what frightened me.
And I remember pointing to that figure.
And I said in German, lady, lady, calling that woman the lady.
And then my mother realized what had made me so afraid.
And she removed it.
I think I must have been about-- perhaps just under two.
And I remember that.
And I also remember my push chair.
I've got an incredible memory going back, but what happened yesterday is a bit more difficult to remember.
I remember being in the push chair and being given some little chocolates, which were very unusual.
They were all individually wrapped ones.
You call them Neapolitans now.
And my mother pinpointed that story when I told her many years later.
She pinpointed that I must have been about two and a half, because an uncle-- Uncle [?
Moritz, ?] one of her brothers-- had come on a visit from America and had given them to me.
Because they weren't-- wasn't the usual sort of chocolate one could get in those days.
And I remember that, sitting in the push chair.
I remember each house that we lived in.
And I remember the possible times when we moved.
I pinpointed with my year when I started school, with the year that I was a little bridesmaid together with my cousin there.
I must've been five.
In Germany, you start school at six years of age.
You mean, you want to know about the house where we lived in last?
That was called Number 9 [?
Mariahilferstrasse ?] in Munich.
And it was a Catholic area.
Munich is Catholic.
And not far, within, within a minute or two, there was a big, round place with a church in the center.
And the place roundabout was used twice a year for fairs, which we loved very much.
And we always used to go there.
And I remember, I used to have, when I was little, quite a lot of non-Jewish little playmates.
And we, we used to play.
And, and that was until they weren't allowed to play with us anymore.
I'm talking about the-- that was actually the last, the last years.
I was 15 when I left.
And we lived there quite a few years.
But I also remember the places before then.
Yes, I remember that.
The episode now I'm talking about just comes back to mind.
Because it didn't happen from the last place we lived, because I was older then.
But when I was about six and seven, we moved to a place very near there.
And it also, it seemed to be around the corner.
That was [?
Schweiger-Strasse ?] Number 10.
And it also pertained to the same church and the same playground.
And that must have been-- those are my earlier memories from a six to 12-year-old or six to 10-year-old.
What age, you mean?
Six to 10-- it was a-- we had the business there as well.
Leather goods, and my parents worked very hard.
It was the-- just before the '30s, the end of 1920s.
And times were very tough.
I know my parents worked very hard.
And that was the home.
There were two rooms which were taken off for the business.
And then we only-- I remember we only had one large bedroom then.
And that was before my sister was born.
My brother was born three and 1/2 years after me.
My sister's exactly seven years and a day younger than myself.
And apart from that, there was just a kitchen and a living room and one bedroom.
Because the other two rooms were taken over for the business.
But the funniest thing is, I, I always remember.
We had a bathroom, which was quite unusual in those days.
Not every house had a bathroom.
There was always running water.
Not hot and cold, but running water, of course, and a bathroom.
Now that, I do remember.
Yes.
Yes.
Probably around and about were more non-Jewish friends before I went to school.
After I went to school, I went to a Jewish school.
And then I had many Jewish friends.
Oh, it wasn't my first.
It was my first and last school.
I went right through school age.
Yes, I, I remember it very well.
It's called the [GERMAN] Folkshul.
And one thing I must tell you about that one.

Shouldn't wash one's dirty linen in public, but although it was a Jewish school, we children who came from Polish extraction, meaning, although I was born in Germany, my parents were-- my mother was there since she was a baby.
My father straight after the First World War, who he fought for Austria, Germany.
He came from Galicia, Poland.
And my mother was born in Warsaw.
But when she was a little girl, she came with her family to Nuremberg, so which made us, actually, in the German mind, Polish-- from Polish extraction.
But not only in the German mind-- also among the Jews, there was a big division.
I don't know if you know about that.
It's rather sad.
And I got very angry a few weeks ago.
Two wonderful books came out-- I believe I showed you one before, which depicts the history of my family in great detail.
And all the other people-- most of the other people it depicts, I know about myself.
A few weeks ago-- and that's at the [GERMAN] in Munchen-- the East Jews, the Polish Jews in Munich.
So I, well, I wasn't that happy with it.
But because my family appeared so-- and all my, my cousins and everybody is in there.
So I thought, well, that was the East German Jews in Munich.
A few weeks ago, I was sent from Germany a wonderful book-- again, quite a heavy book.
And it also said, But guess what?
Those were the yekkes, the German Jews in Munich.
And it-- I was so upset that, after 60 years, the division still seems to persist in some way, although it's watered down quite a lot.
But in those days, they did not mix.
And our teachers, they were all Orthodox Jews.
And they were all German Jews.
And in a way, we Polish children were I want to say-- well, more or less neglected education-wise, I would say.
Well, I, I never realized my potential at school.
And I know that now.
Because after the age of 12, 11 or 12, when we went into the higher school-- it wasn't allowed to be called a high school.
But we had the curriculum of a grammar school.
But because we were Jewish, it was already nasty times.
So that was-- you know, it was still called a folkshul-- for the, for the people, people's school.
It wasn't sort of allowed.
We learned French and English.
But that wasn't-- officially, we were a folkshul.
And there, we had a wonderful teacher.
He was also German-Jewish.
But he didn't make any difference.
He didn't differentiate between the Polish and the German-Jewish children.
Now, we only spoke Hochdeutsch at home.
I mean, it wasn't that-- it wasn't a case of when you say the Poles speaking Yiddish or anything like that.
My German-- that was the only thing I was really good at in school.
I was never good at anything else much, except physical training and German.
Until then, until I changed into the class, I was the-- almost the bottom of the class in everything.
I had a teacher who really-- you know, I, I felt I was handicapped.
I was right at the bottom.
And suddenly, suddenly, I was the top of the class in German.
I still remember my poems that I learned as a child off my heart, taking 10, 20 minutes to recite each poem.
I still know them off my heart.
But all of a sudden, that talent of mine was brought to the forefront, whereas it had been pushed and suppressed in the lower classes with the other teachers.
It's, it's, it's, it's really a pity.
So I'm telling you, I was angry when I got this new book showing the German Jews of Munich, as opposed to the Polish Jews.
And I don't think that ought to be sort of pinpointed, even today.
But there is still a little bit, yes.
There is-- there seems to be still that separation.
There was never a chance, very seldom a possibility, of intermarriage between the Poles and the, and the yekkes.
Did you know that?
It's quite amazing, isn't it?
In fact, I've got a very good friend, a school friend I came over with to England-- went to school together, came over to England together.
Her mother, being pure Jewish-German, was disinherited because she married a Pole, a Polish Jew.
So that division-- and we felt it as schoolchildren.
I would say about half.
About 30 children, yes.
I think so.
I've got one friend.
He's in America now.
He lives in, in Beverly Hills.
He's still very bitter about it.
And you know, at the time, I don't think we took quite as much notice.
We just felt we seem to be the more stupid ones, which we really weren't.
And I'm beginning to realize now that all the school reports which were pretty awful weren't really true.
We were Orthodox, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the whole family.
Even today, all my cousins-- I must also say, I grew up with a lot of cousins.
The whole family seemed to live in Munich, on my father's side.
My mother's side was in Nuremberg.
And they mostly emigrated to America.
My father's side all seemed to live in Munich.
And they all seem to have emigrated en masse to Israel before the worst of the Hitler times.
And we were a very close-knit family.
Orthodox.
And we went to the, the synagogue which was also-- even the synagogues were segregated.
There was the German-Jewish synagogue.
There was the Polish synagogue mainly, to which we belonged.
And then there were sort of little splinter groups according to your background.
And now, I'm beginning to realize how segregated really we were, that we didn't make friendships.
Perhaps in school, we might have made some friendships.
But they never went home.
They never went as far as birthday parties or anything, which is a shame, really.
Perhaps I shouldn't have mentioned it.
But it's a fact.
It was a fact of our life.
And it shouldn't have been.
Oh, properly, yes.
Yes.
I'll also tell you something.
Because we had-- after the '30s, beginning after '33, business became very difficult, leather business and things.
My father used to go traveling.
And in fact, the, the rest of the family was also in a similar business.
My parents were sort of more on the lower financial level to some of my uncles and aunts there.
And so my mother had a, an idea.
She said, look.
Business isn't working.
Let's do something completely different.
She said, let's do something where we get Jewish customers.
And she thought of starting a laundry.
It's quite an unusual thing, really.
And they decided that.
And they started a laundry.
It was called [INAUDIBLE] Engelhard in 25 [INAUDIBLE] Munich.
And like she predicted, we had to a lot of Jewish customers.
Because then the Nazi era started, and people-- Jewish people wanted to help other Jews.
We were sort of-- we also had then German-Jewish customers.
I mean, the division didn't go into business like that.
And we had a lot of customers.

And then as the Hitler times got worse, the Nazis-- we used to have them come in sometimes in a German uniform around the back of the shop-- it had a back entrance-- and pick up their laundry and support us as well.
That's-- not to home.
No, no, we had a proper shop, proper business.
And we didn't have a big laundry.
It was sort of-- what happened was we accepted it, sorted it and that.
And then it was sent to a very big laundry for the actual washing.
Then it came back.
It was dried and then through a hot mangle and ironed.
We had women ironing and finishing it off.
And then my father used to-- he had a little, a little van.
And he used to deliver it.
And I used to deliver on my bicycle.
I was a terror on my bike.
I had a bike.
The first-- I had the first bicycle in the whole school.
And my parents weren't very popular because of that, because the children nagged, the other children.
And they all wanted bikes.
And for, for about a whole year, I was the most popular girl in school.
[LAUGHS] Because they all learned to ride on my little bike.
So as I got bigger, I helped in the business after school.
And, and business did quite well.
We were never rich.
But I always remember my parents working very hard.
And you asked me about Shabbat.
Now, that's what I wanted to tell you.
The shop also had a little kitchen.
And my mother used to, on a Friday, she used to cook and bake all the things in the shop.
And because you were very careful of the, then non-Jews then.
They were very. you know, very jealous.
Whatever you cooked-- my mother was a wonderful cook.
She could make some wonderful smelling soup and, and things.
I mean, chicken was a rarity.
You didn't have chicken unless it was Yom Tov time, unless it was the festival times.
But anything she cooked, it smelled very nice.
And the women at work, they had a, an hour for lunch.
And she used to bake there.
And if she baked one cake, she used to make another two that they didn't know about.
And people didn't have ovens in those days.
So you used to send your cakes to the bakers, who was around the corner.
I don't know if you've heard that story before.
Very few people had their own ovens for baking in.
So my mother used to roll out the cakes quickly and put the-- put everything in it.
And it was taken around the corner to the bakers.
And in the evening, we fetched it home.
And so the workers didn't really know what was being cooked and what we had.
We had to be careful, because the, the people were poor in those days.
And it was already the times when they said, the Jews eat like this, and the Jews have that.
Yes.
They were non-Jewish, sure.
And they were very nice.
But my mother always used to give them a taste of this and a-- or a piece of cake and that.
But they never knew exactly what she cooked or baked.
Because that was sort of hidden as far as we could.
And then, in the evening, we took it home in the little, the little van.
Traditional, very nice.
With candles-- the candles were burning.
Papa made kiddish.
And we had our own friends.
We didn't go to synagogue Friday night.
But we went every Saturday morning.
And that was really the meeting place.
That was the, the relaxation for Jewish people.
They used to meet in the synagogue, meet their friends.
Later on, it became almost dangerous to leave, because--
Now I'm talking about the, the time just before we left-- 1937, 1938.
It became quite dangerous to congregate outside the synagogues when you left, when you left, especially on a weekday.
When Yom Tov in which the festivals fell on a weekday, our rabbi used to be very protective-- not protective.
He used to be quite angry with people if they congregated, like people do outside, waiting for each other.
And he would stand by the entrance and let only about 10 people out, mingle with the crowd, and then the next.
And he also was quite angry with the ladies, to which my mother did not belong, because she never dressed ostentatiously.
But, you know, it is-- I don't know if it's a Jewish trend or not.
I mean, you like to dress nicely on Shabbat and on Yom Tov.
And, and some of the ladies looked perhaps a bit too smart for the times that were.
And the rabbi used to beg of them to dress modestly.
But you'll see on the photograph.
Actually, the hat that my mother wears on the photograph which you'll see later, was the same hat as she used to wear on, on Sabbath and on, on festival.
And she was, you know, dressed nicely but modestly.
She was a good-looking woman.
And a lot of people didn't observe that rule, to dress low.
And the rabbi got rather upset and begged us.
I don't think anybody can visualize what it was like after 1933, '34.
To live. you, you were supposed to live as low as possible, not to, you know, not to show that you were Jewish or anything.
And, and that's the only time it was ignored, was on the Sabbath and on Yom Tov, on the festivals, by a lot of the people.
I remember one-- I remember one thing, that people were discussing it.
Parents and, and even the children at school were discussing about the, the chancellor, Hitler becoming chancellor.
And just before, I remember my parents saying, oh, it will never happen.
It can't happen.
I mean, it's impossible.
But happen, it did.
We didn't have a radio.
My parents didn't like the idea of a radio with the martial music blaring.
And we didn't have a newspaper, either.
Because what can you see in a newspaper?
It was all, you know, pertaining to Hitler and his, and his gang.
And so we didn't bother.
So we had, we had the-- something that was very much like the [GERMAN].
What was it called?
I don't know-- the Jewish Munich paper.
We had that.
And we got quite a lot of news.
Now, I remember something about that, yes.
It had a column.
And it said [GERMAN].
That means, join the Jews and left Judaism.
So at one time, in the beginning-- I was quite a little then.
And I remember reading about, reading the names and that.
And there were a few people who became Jewish, mostly because they married in.
And there were a few names.
But later on, after '33, '34, '35, you had nothing but column after column [GERMAN].
A fat lot it helped them.
It didn't help at all, because Hitler told you it was a Jew.
But there were lots and lots of people who thought that they could save themselves by opting out.
I think that's the word, isn't it, opting out.
We went to school.
We didn't feel as badly as some of my friends tell me who went to non-Jewish schools.
Now, they suffered a lot more.
We didn't have that.
My own memories are normal about those days.
Because I remember that children used to run after us and call us dirty Jews.
And you know, some started fights and all that.
But on the whole, we didn't suffer like the children who went to non-Jewish schools.
It-- that passed us by.
We experienced anti-Semitism, yes.
And we experienced-- as it got-- progressed, as the years went by, it became worse and worse about, you know, [GERMAN], don't buy from Jews.
And-- but by the time, my parents had changed the business.
And we, we didn't do too badly.
Because like I said, we had Jewish customers.
And-- but, actually, being attacked didn't happen often, only from some gangs of other school children.
We went to school.
And, also, the school seemed to stagger their times a little bit.
I think we started slightly different times than the ordinary schools, which, which helped.
But, now I remember walking to school.
I mean, there was no such thing as riding on the bus.
And that's probably why I got my bicycle.
And we walked.
We met our friends on corners and different roads.
And we walked to school.
And our school hours were, like I said, the same as a grammar school, till 1:30.
Now, there were a lot of Jewish parents who were working, businesses perhaps or had to go out to work.
And what you do with the children after half past 1:00?
There was a wonderful institution, which is German.
And I think England's only just now about to catch up on it.
It was-- I don't, I don't think they've ever really caught up on that system.
It's called a [INAUDIBLE].
That's an after school.
We had a Jewish [INAUDIBLE] next to the school.
It had a little bit of a stigma attached to it.
So the-- mostly the Polish Jewish children, who were a little bit less affluent than the German-Jewish children, they went to the [INAUDIBLE], where they were very nicely looked after.
We had lunch there, cooked on the premises, very nice.
And then we did our homework under supervision.
There were people called [GERMAN], who, whose job it was to supervise the homework and look after us.
And there we stayed till about 5:00.
And we went off to cheder.
